item.title: Enumerated type
item.description:
Enumerated type #

An enumerated type is a finite set of alternative values. This is a more readable alternative to so-called “magic numbers”.

Example. A traffic light can have three colors: green, yellow and red.

We could model this with a “magic number”. For instance, a variable x with type integer, where:

  • x < 0 means green,
  • x == 0 means yellow,
  • x > 0 means red.

Instead, we can declare an enumerated type (for instance named Light) with 3 possible values (Green, Yellow and Red), and use a variable of this type. Then instead of

item.title: Fold
item.description:
Fold #

A variety of operations on a list can be understood as combining its values inductively, either from left to right or from right to left.

Example. Consider the list

$\qquad 1, 2, 3, 4$

The sum of its elements can be computed inductively as:

  • $((1 + 2) + 3) + 4\qquad$ (left-to-right), or
  • $1 + (2 + (3 + 4))\qquad$ (right-to-left)

Similarly, the least element in this list can be computed as

item.title: Linear recursion
item.description:
Linear recursion #

Definition. A recursive function/method is linear recursive if it performs at most one recursive call each time it is executed.

Hint. In an imperative language (like Java), a linear recursive algorithm can easily be transformed into an iterative one (i.e. an algorithm that uses only loops).

Haskell does not admit loops, so linear recursion is the standard way to perform simple iterations (although list comprehensions can be used instead in certain cases). Accordingly, in this section, we will mostly use Haskell as an illustration.

item.title: Organization
item.description:
Organization #

The course consists of:

  • 20 lectures (40 hours), and
  • 10 labs (20 hours).

Julien Corman will teach the lectures and labs.

Schedule #

Lectures #

By default, lectures are scheduled on:

  • Tuesday 15:30 - 17:30
  • Thursday 10:30 - 12:30

Hint. Lectures can be followed via Teams (e.g. for students sitting at the back of the class), by joining the corresponding meeting (in the “Lectures” channel of this course’s Team).

item.title: Paradigm
item.description:
Paradigm #

A programming paradigm is a way to write programs. Paradigms include:

  • imperative programming,
  • object-oriented programming,
  • functional programming,
  • logical programming,
  • etc.

Programming languages are often categorized based on the paradigms that they support.

Disclaimer. The boundaries of a paradigm are not always clear, and can be subjective. For this reason, we will (cautiously) borrow descriptions from Wikipedia.

Imperative programming #

Languages #

The most widely used programming languages are imperative.

item.title: Set, list, tuple
item.description:
Set, list, tuple #

Set #

A set can be (informally) viewed as a collection of elements with no duplicate and in no specific order.

Examples.

  • $\{2,5\}$ and $\{5,2\}$ are the same set,
  • $\{2,5,2\}$ is not a set.

Power set #

Definition. The power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ of a set $X$ is the set of all subsets of $X$.

item.title: Tokens
item.description:
Tokens #

Syntax. Here are syntactic constraints on basic elements of a Haskell program:

meaning syntax examples
variable name starts with a lower case letter x, f, remainder, maxValue
function name starts with a lower case letter square, applyBoolOp
type starts with an upper case letter Integer, Char, Bool
type variable starts with a lower case letter a, type, baseType
constructor starts with a capital letter Cons, CityC, True

item.title: Type variable
item.description:
Type variable #

A (regular) variable stands for an underspecified value. Similarly, a type variable stands for an underspecified type.

Benefit. Type variables let us write functions/methods that apply to different types, without compromising type safety.

Example. Consider a function half that maps a list to its first half (say rounded down if the list has odd length).

The implementation of this function is identical for:

  • a list of strings,
  • a list of integers,
  • a list of Boolean values,
  • etc.

A type variable lets us declare such a function

item.title: Base types
item.description:
Base types #

A base type in Haskell intuitively represents a set of values (like in Java, and many other languages).

Here are a few native base types:

type meaning syntax of constants in Haskell
Char a Unicode character, like b, B, 5, _ or \t. 'b', 'B', '5', '_' or '\t' (single quotes)
String a sequence of Unicode characters, like banana or f?#\t5 "banana" or "f?#\t5" (double quotes)
Bool a Boolean value True or False
Integer an arbitrary integer (i.e. an element of $\mathbb{Z}$) -42, 0 or 3
Int an integer within a certain range, from $- 2^{63}$ to $2^{63} -1$ (i.e. 64 bits) by default. -42, 0 or 3
Float a 32 bits floating-point number -42, 4472, 4472.0 or 4472.1357
Double a 64 bits floating-point number -42, 4472, 4472.0, 4472.1357 or 4472.13595499958

item.title: Compile time vs runtime error
item.description:
Compile time vs runtime error #

Definition. In (pre)compiled languages (like Java, C/C++, C#, Haskell, Python, Go, Rust, etc.):

  • A compile time error prevents a program from compiling. It can be thought of as a “syntactic” error.
  • A runtime error (also called a bug) occurs during the execution of a program. It may depend on the input of the program.

Note. An IDE can identify some compile time errors (and highlight them), without even attempting to compile the program.

item.title: Material
item.description:
Course material #

Lectures #

This website contains the lecture material.

Additional readings are listed in the dedicated section. We emphasize that these are optional (the course is self-contained).

Assignments #

Assignments will be released via Codeboard.
Details are provided in the dedicated section.

item.title: Pattern matching
item.description:
Pattern matching #

Pattern matching is a traditional feature of functional languages. It pairs well with sum types (which we will see later).

Pattern matching is no longer exclusive to functional languages: several imperative languages now support it to some extent (natively or via libraries). This includes:

  • C# (since 2020),
  • Java (since 2021),
  • PHP (since 2021),
  • Python (since 2021)
  • Rust (since its first release in 2015).

in Haskell #

A pattern is a syntactic expression that can match certain values.

item.title: Product type
item.description:
Product type #

A product type can be viewed as a simplistic class, and its instances as simplistic objects.

in Haskell #

Syntax. Like an enumerated type, a product type in Haskell can be declared with the keyword data, as follows

data <product type name> = <definition>

There are two alternative syntaxes for this <definition>:

  • a tuple type, or
  • a definition with a constructor.

Definition as a tuple type #

Example. The declaration below defines a product type Interval.

item.title: Anonymous function
item.description:
Anonymous function #

We have seen in the dedicated section that a function can be written anonymously, as a lambda expression.

Anonymous functions are a traditional feature of functional languages (like Haskell). But they have also been integrated to a variety of imperative languages, for instance:

  • Python (since 1994),
  • Javascript (since its initial release in 1995),
  • C# (since 2007)
  • C++ (since 2011)
  • Java (since 2014)
  • Rust (since its initial release in 2015)

In Haskell #

Syntax. “$\lambda x.$” in Haskell is written \x ->

item.title: Currying
item.description:
Currying #

Functional program traditionally define functions in curried form (for a definition, we refer to the dedicated section).

In Haskell #

Syntax #

A Haskell function can (in theory) take a tuple as argument.

Example.

power :: (Int, Int) -> Int
power (x,y) = x^y

However, functions in curried form are usually preferred, because they are more versatile.

Example. The function above in curried form can be written:

power :: Int -> (Int -> Int)
power = \x -> (\y -> x^y)

or equivalently:

item.title: Function
item.description:
Function #

A function $f\colon X \to Y$ maps each element $x$ of its domain $X$ to an element $f(x)$ of its codomain $Y$.

Terminology. A function is also called a map.

Examples.

  • $\ $the function $f\colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ defined by

$\qquad f(x) = x^2$

$\qquad$maps each integer to its squared value.
$\qquad$For instance,

$\qquad f(3) = 9$

  • $\ $the function $g\colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ defined by

$\qquad g(x) = \{y \mid y \in \mathbb{N},\ y < x\}$

item.title: Native functions
item.description:
Native functions #

Syntax. Many native functions/operators (e.g. over numbers or Boolean values) have the same syntax in Haskell and Java. This includes equality (==), sum (+), product (*), etc.

We highlight below a few cases where the two syntaxes differ:

meaning syntax in Haskell example syntax in Java example
inequality /= x /= 2 != x != 2
negation (NOT) not not x ! !x
disjunction (OR) || x || y || (lazy evaluation) or
| (full evaluation)		 x || y or
x | y
conjunction (AND) && x && y && (lazy evaluation) or
& (full evaluation)		 x && y or
x & y

item.title: Reading path
item.description:
Reading path #

This page lists the topics covered during each lecture. It will be updated after a lecture.

Advice. If you miss a lecture, make sure to read the sections that we covered (and do the related exercises) before the next lecture. Otherwise you may fall behind.

Warning. On this website, the material is grouped in thematic chapters, to ease your revisions for the exam. These chapters do not always reflect the order of the lectures.

item.title: Recursive type
item.description: Recursive types #

item.title: Sum type
item.description:
Sum type #

A sum type represents several alternative types.

in Haskell #

Syntax. A sum type in Haskell is declared as

data <sum type name> = <first type definition> | ... | <last type definition>

Example. In the following declarations:

  • Point is a product type, and
  • Shape is a sum type.
-- a point in the Euclidean plane, represented by its coordinates
data Point = Point Double Double

-- a shape, defined as either a triangle or a rectangle
data Shape = Triangle Point Point Point | Rectangle Point Point Point Point

Note. in Haskell, an enumerated type is a sum type.

item.title: Termination
item.description:
Recursion and termination #

Tail recursive function #

In Haskell #

Exercise.

The function failedReverse below seems similar to the function reverse seen in the previous section.

However, there is something wrong with it. Can you identify what it is?

failedReverse :: [Int] -> [Int]
failedReverse [] = []
failedReverse (x : xs) = failedReverse (xs ++ [x])

main :: IO ()
main = print $ failedReverse [1,2,3]

Solution.

The recursive call (Line XXX) is performed on a list that is not smaller than the input list.

item.title: Type class in Haskell
item.description:
Type classes #

TODO: write something

TODO: move this to the previous section?

Type classes #

Generify a function #

item.title: Typing
item.description:
Typing #

A typing system restricts the values that can be used in certain contexts.

Example. In Java, the following instruction is incorrect, because the variable x has type int, whereas the value "Hi" is a string.

int x = "Hi";

This line of code would generate a compile-time error.

Terminology. In this example, int is called a type annotation.

Static vs dynamic typing #

Static typing #

Definition. A programming language is statically typed if type checks are performed at compile time.

item.title: Boolean condition
item.description:
Boolean condition #

Pattern matching has limitations.

Examples. No pattern can match:

  • integers greater than 2,
  • odd integers,
  • etc.

For more expressive conditions, programming language usually rely on Boolean expressions.

in Haskell #

Boolean expression #

We have seen how to define a Boolean operator (precisely, the Boolean operator “AND”) with patterns in Haskell.

This is not necessary: Haskell provides native implementations, similar to their counterparts in Java (and many programming languages). They include:

item.title: Evaluation
item.description:
Evaluation #

Evaluation for this course is based on:

  • a written exam, and
  • 5 to 6 assignments with oral presentations.

The assignments are optional.

Marking scheme #

An assignment counts only if your mark for this assignment is superior to your mark at the written exam.
If this is the case, then the assignment counts for 5% of your final mark.

Example. For the marks reported on the right:

item.title: Pipeline
item.description:
Pipeline #

Motivation #

Syntax. Consider a chain of function applications, where the output of each function is the (unique) input to the next function in the chain. For instance

$\qquad h(g(f(x)))$

Computationally, this reads from right to left :

$\qquad$ “start with $x$, then apply $f$ to it, then apply $g$ to the result, and then $h$ to the result”.

Instead, when we write code, it can be convenient to use an expression that mimics the order of execution (when read from left to right).

item.title: Prerequisites
item.description:
Prerequisites #

Students are expected to have completed the Computer Programming course (INF/01 76203).

Consequence. The following topics are not covered in this course:

  • variables, assignments, variable scope,
  • basic data types (int, char, boolean, etc.).
  • expression evaluation,
  • conditional statements (“if/then/else”) and loops,
  • methods/functions,
  • arrays,
  • basic syntax of Java,
  • etc.

Hint. If you have not completed this course, you can ask for office hours to get help on these topics.

item.title: Pure function
item.description:
Pure function #

Functional languages like Haskell favor pure functions, which behave like mathematical functions.

In imperative code, their adoption varies by language and community. For instance, pure functions are not idiomatic in C, but common in languages like Java, JavaScript, Python or Rust.

Side effect #

Definition. A method/function has a side effect if it modifies a resource (variable, object, array, etc.) that is defined outside of its scope.

item.title: Tuple
item.description:
Tuple #

Syntax. A tuple in Haskell is written in parentheses.

Example. The pair $(\mathit{banana}, 5)$ is written

("banana", 5)

Syntax. The type of a tuple is written in parentheses (like the tuple itself).

Examples.

Haskell tuple Possible type
(5,3) (Int, Int)
("banana", 5) (String, Int)
("banana", 5, "tomato") (String, Int, String)

item.title: Function
item.description:
Function #

Function definition #

Syntax. A function in Haskell is defined analogously to a mathematical function, with the symbol =.

Example. The function $f(x) = 2x$ is written in Haskell

f x = 2 * x

Warning. As opposed to Java (and C/C++, C#, Javascript, Python, etc.), the symbol = in Haskell does not assign a value to a variable.

Syntax. Note that $f(x)$ is written f x, without parentheses. This also holds when applying a function to a constant.

item.title: Functor
item.description:
Functor #

The notion of functor comes from a branch of mathematics called category theory.

For programmers, a functor can be viewed as a parameterized type that implements a certain function, while respecting certain constraints.

In Haskell #

Notation. We will use t in this section to refer to a Haskell functor.

Definition. A functor in Haskell is a parameterized type t that implements a function

$\qquad$ fmap :: (a -> b) -> t a -> t b

item.title: Generic type
item.description:
Generic type #

In Haskell #

Syntax #

We have seen how Haskell uses constructors to define composite types.

Example (reminder). As we have seen earlier, the type of lists of integers can be defined as follows:

data IntList = Nil | Cons Int (IntList)

Similarly, the type of lists of Booleans can be defined as

data BoolList = Nil | Cons Bool (BoolList)

If we want to make this more generic, we can use a type variable instead of the concrete type (Int or Bool here).

item.title: Monad
item.description:
Monad #

Like functors, monads come from category theory.

For programmers, a monad can be viewed as a functor that implements additional functions, while respecting certain constraints.

In Haskell #

We have seen in the previous section that a functor is a parameterized type that implements the function fmap.

A monad is a functor that implements two additional functions, called return and join.

Example. We have seen how the parameterized type “List” (written [ ] in Haskell) can be made a functor by implementing fmap.

item.title: Office hours
item.description:
Office hours #

You can ask for office hours to get help with:

  • prerequisites for this course,
  • an assignment,
  • a notion seen during a lecture,
  • etc.

These appointments must be scheduled via email or Teams.

Additional content #

Office hours can also be organized on demand for a group of students, on any of the following topics:

  • hierarchical file system,
  • git,
  • using a terminal,
  • installing/updating/deleting software on Windows, macOS and Linux,
  • text editors and IDEs (landscape and usage).

This content is optional. It is meant to help you interact with a computer, so that your learning experience does not get impaired by technological obstacles.

item.title: Content
item.description:
Content of the course #

This is a programming course for beginners, with an emphasis on:

  • (class-based) object-oriented programming and
  • functional programming.

Programming languages #

We will use:

  • Haskell at the beginning of this course, for a short introduction to functional programming, and
  • Java during the rest of the course, for object-oriented and more general techniques.

Note. Many notions seen in this course are not specific to Haskell or Java. Accordingly, some sections may use pseudocode for conciseness.

item.title: List comprehension
item.description:
List comprehension #

Pure functional programming does not admit loops.

In particular, this is the case of Haskell. So iteration in Haskell is primarily achieved via recursive functions.

However, Haskell also provides a convenient syntax for a limited form of iteration, called list comprehensions.

List comprehensions are also available in Python (with a slightly different syntax) since the release of Python 2 (in 2000).

Syntax #

We have already seen set comprehension.

item.title: Option type
item.description:
Option type #

We introduce an important generic and composite type, available in many languages, under a variety of names (Maybe, Option, optional, Optional, Nullable, etc.)

We will see in the dedicated sections that such a type can also be made a functor, and (perhaps most importantly) a monad. For now, we will use it without these extra features.

Motivation #

Problem. A programming language may not have a type that exactly fits the domain of a function that we want to implement.

item.title: Program
item.description:
Program #

File #

A simple Haskell program is written in a file with the extension .hs. For instance, MyProgram.hs.

Content #

Functions #

A Haskell program primarily consists of function definitions.

Hint. The order of function definitions in a program is irrelevant.

The function main #

To make a program executable, one of its functions must be the entry point. By default, this function is called main, and has type IO ().

item.title: Additional resources
item.description:
Additional resources #

This course is self-contained, meaning that no extra reading is required.
We list here optional resources that may help you complement it, or provide a different perspective.

A reading list for this course is available in the catalogue of the Unibz’s library. All books can be accessed via the library (either physically or digitally).

Among these books, we highlight the following five.

Introductory textbooks #

Java #

Java : the complete reference (11th edition) by Herbert Schildt McGraw-Hill Education, 2019

item.title: List
item.description:
List #

Recursive form #

Lists in Haskell are recursive.

Definition (finite list in Haskell).

  • [] is the empty list,
  • if xs is a list and x an element, then x:xs is a list.

In addition, all elements in a list must have the same type. If this type is a, then the list has type [a].

Example. The Haskell list

$\qquad$5 : (2 : [])

item.title: Software
item.description:
Recommended software #

Lectures #

No particular software is required for the lectures.

The exercises can be solved using web applications, for instance:

  • the Haskell playground and
  • the Java playground

However, an IDE may be more comfortable.

Assignments #

For the assignments, you will need an IDE for Haskell and Java (possibly the same one).

We provide elementary instructions below.

Further details will be provided during the first lab (dedicated to software-related questions for this course).
This page will be released after the dedicated lecture.
